column2: Related Use case
column3: Pre-conditions
column4: Test Description (steps)
column5: Expected Outcome

column2: Auth registration success
column3: FastAPI backend running; Supabase Auth and public.users mapping configured
column4: 1. Send POST /auth/register with valid name, email, and password. 2. Capture response token payload. 3. Verify matching profile row is created in public.users.
column5: HTTP 201 is returned with valid JWT/auth payload, and a mirrored public.users row exists for the same user so the account can log in immediately.

column2: Auth registration rollback on profile mirror failure
column3: Backend running; a database constraint or fixture exists that causes public.users insert to fail after auth creation
column4: 1. Send POST /auth/register with input that triggers profile-table failure. 2. Observe API response. 3. Attempt to log in with the same credentials afterward.
column5: Registration fails cleanly, the auth footprint is rolled back, and the same email is not left in a half-created locked state.

column2: Auth login success and last-login update
column3: Registered user exists; backend and Supabase available
column4: 1. Send POST /auth/login with valid credentials. 2. Verify access token and refresh token are returned. 3. Inspect users.last_login_at for the account.
column5: HTTP 200 is returned with active tokens, and last_login_at is updated for the user record.

column2: Auth login invalid credentials
column3: Registered user exists; backend running
column4: 1. Send POST /auth/login with wrong password. 2. Verify response status and message. 3. Confirm no session is created.
column5: A client/auth error is returned, no JWT is issued, and the system does not expose sensitive internal details.

column2: Protected report upload success
column3: Backend running; valid JWT bearer token available; Supabase storage bucket configured
column4: 1. Send POST /upload/report or POST /reports/upload with valid bearer token, user_id, and a valid PDF. 2. Capture returned storage path and public URL. 3. Verify the object is stored in the medical-reports bucket under an isolated user scope.
column5: Upload succeeds and returns created storage metadata, with the report stored in the expected user-scoped location.

column2: Protected report upload rejects unauthenticated request
column3: Backend running; no valid JWT token supplied
column4: 1. Call the protected upload endpoint without Authorization header or with an invalid token. 2. Inspect response. 3. Verify no object is written to storage.
column5: The request is rejected with 401 or 403, and no report object is created.

column2: Async ingestion accepted immediately
column3: Backend running; OCR and Gemini dependencies configured; valid user and file available
column4: 1. Send POST /reports/ingest with user_id and report file. 2. Measure initial response timing. 3. Capture report_id from the response.
column5: HTTP 202 is returned quickly with a report_id, while OCR and extraction continue in the background without blocking the client.

column2: Report listing pagination and newest-first sorting
column3: Authenticated user has multiple uploaded reports with different timestamps
column4: 1. Call GET /reports with pagination parameters. 2. Verify page size and ordering. 3. Compare results against stored reports for the same user.
column5: Only the authenticated user's reports are returned, paginated correctly, and ordered from newest to oldest.

column2: Report status lifecycle tracking
column3: An async ingestion job is in progress or recently completed
column4: 1. Call GET /reports/status/{report_id} after ingest starts. 2. Poll until the pipeline completes. 3. Track processing_status values and terminal payload.
column5: Status progresses through documented states such as pending, ocr_complete, and done or failed, and the final response matches the stored processing state.

column2: Synchronous report processing success
column3: Backend running; OCR and Gemini configured; valid report file and user_id available
column4: 1. Send POST /reports/process with multipart file, user_id, and extraction mode. 2. Wait for full response. 3. Inspect OCR and extraction fields in the payload.
column5: The endpoint blocks until upload, OCR, and extraction finish, then returns a complete success payload with report identifiers and extracted outputs.

column2: OCR on stored report succeeds and persists text
column3: Backend running; uploaded report already exists for the user
column4: 1. Send POST /reports/ocr with valid user_id and report_id. 2. Verify OCR text and confidence are produced. 3. Confirm medical_reports row is updated.
column5: OCR completes successfully, persists ocr_text and confidence to medical_reports, and returns a success response without requiring re-upload.

column2: OCR rejects invalid report or mismatched user
column3: Backend running; report_id is invalid or belongs to another user
column4: 1. Send POST /reports/ocr with invalid or unauthorized report_id. 2. Observe response. 3. Confirm no OCR update is written.
column5: The request fails with a client error, and no OCR text or downstream indexing is persisted.

column2: Gemini lab extraction success with normalization and idempotent replace
column3: Backend running; report has OCR text; Gemini API key configured
column4: 1. Send POST /reports/extract-labs-gemini for a report with OCR text. 2. Inspect inserted lab_results. 3. Re-run the same endpoint for the same report.
column5: Extraction succeeds, values and units are normalized, all detected tests are inserted, and a re-run replaces previous lab_results for that report instead of duplicating them.

column2: Gemini extraction failure path with retries and no partial insert
column3: Backend running; test fixture or config forces Gemini/extraction failure
column4: 1. Trigger POST /reports/extract-labs-gemini on a report that causes model failure or malformed output. 2. Check retry behavior in logs or response. 3. Inspect lab_results table afterward.
column5: The service retries up to the documented limit, surfaces an error clearly if all attempts fail, and does not leave partial or duplicate lab_results inserted.

column2: Alert evaluation success and idempotent replacement
column3: Backend running; user has lab_results that trigger alerts
column4: 1. Send POST /alerts/evaluate/{user_id}. 2. Inspect created alerts and alert_evidence rows. 3. Run the same endpoint again.
column5: Alerts are generated from the deterministic rules, linked to evidence, and the second run replaces prior alert outputs rather than creating duplicates.

column2: Alert retrieval returns explainable evidence
column3: Backend running; alerts already exist for the user
column4: 1. Call GET /alerts/{user_id}. 2. Inspect alert list, severity, and evidence payload. 3. Verify evidence references the triggering lab results or contextual data.
column5: Stored alerts are returned with severity values and explainable evidence data that traces each alert back to the underlying source information.

column2: Rules engine threshold-to-severity mapping
column3: Rules module available; fixtures exist for values near documented thresholds
column4: 1. Evaluate the rules engine with fixture lab values around cutoff boundaries. 2. Compare resulting severities against the documented 13 rules. 3. Repeat for low, medium, and high scenarios.
column5: Each rule maps the input values to the correct severity tier exactly as documented, with no skipped levels or inconsistent threshold handling.

column2: Environment-aware rule escalation
column3: Environmental data exists for the user and trigger thresholds are met
column4: 1. Prepare lab values that already trigger a rule. 2. Add AQI or temperature values above the documented escalation threshold. 3. Re-run alert evaluation.
column5: The base alert is escalated by the documented modifier, and environmental evidence or advisory text is included without bypassing the original deterministic rule check.

column2: OCR text cleaning removes boilerplate conservatively
column3: Sample OCR text contains repeated headers, page markers, and noise but still includes valid medical values
column4: 1. Run the text cleaning pipeline on noisy OCR text. 2. Inspect cleaned output. 3. Compare removed versus preserved sections.
column5: Repeated boilerplate, obvious noise, and spacing artifacts are removed, while medically meaningful values, units, and content remain intact.

column2: Chunking preserves measurement rows as standalone evidence units
column3: Clean OCR text contains table-like lab measurement lines and narrative text
column4: 1. Run doc_to_chunks() or the equivalent chunking entry point. 2. Inspect generated chunks. 3. Verify measurement rows are not merged into unrelated narrative chunks.
column5: Lab measurement lines remain self-contained chunks suitable for citations, while unrelated prose is chunked separately.

column2: Retrieved chunks include citation metadata
column3: Migration 002 applied or metadata backfill logic available; indexed chunks exist
column4: 1. Execute pgvector or FAISS retrieval for a user query. 2. Inspect returned retrieved_chunks fields. 3. Verify filename, source URL, and page metadata where available.
column5: The retrieval response includes contract-compliant citation metadata such as source_filename, source_url, and page_number or a safe nullable fallback.

column2: Section-label filtering and stable top-k ordering
column3: Week-4 retrieval changes applied; indexed chunks span multiple sections
column4: 1. Run retrieval with a section filter. 2. Compare results to an unfiltered query. 3. Verify ranking and top-k behavior remain stable.
column5: Only chunks from the requested section are returned when filtering is applied, and ordering remains relevance-based and deterministic within the configured top-k.

column2: Structured retrieval logging captures latency phases
column3: Week-4 retrieval logging enabled; log output accessible
column4: 1. Execute one retrieval request. 2. Inspect logs for phase timings. 3. Confirm request identifiers and phase names are present.
column5: Logs include structured timing for the documented retrieval phases, plus request identifiers that support observability and debugging.

column2: FAISS retrieval fallback when pgvector is unavailable
column3: Indexed chunk embeddings exist; pgvector path is disabled or unavailable; FAISS dependency installed
column4: 1. Invoke retrieval with FAISS strategy or with pgvector unavailable. 2. Inspect returned chunks and scores. 3. Compare basic relevance against expected results.
column5: Retrieval still returns ranked chunks through the FAISS path without crashing, preserving the same response shape used by callers.

column2: Context schema required-field validation
column3: Context builder inputs available; schema contract file present
column4: 1. Build a context payload missing one required field such as meta.request_id or user_profile.user_id. 2. Validate against the schema. 3. Repeat with a complete payload.
column5: Invalid payloads are rejected by validation, while complete payloads pass and preserve the documented required-versus-optional field behavior.

column2: Context builder size controls
column3: Context builder available; retrieved chunks exceed documented limits
column4: 1. Supply more than 10 chunks or chunks longer than 500 characters. 2. Build the context. 3. Measure final chunk count and total character budget.
column5: The builder enforces the documented constraints of max 10 chunks, max 500 characters per chunk, and approximately 4000 total context characters.

column2: RAG query success for user role with grounding
column3: Backend running; retrieval data exists; Gemini configured
column4: 1. Send POST /api/v1/rag_query with valid user_id, query, and role=user. 2. Inspect answer, context, chunks_retrieved, and grounding flag. 3. Check that citations use the user-facing style.
column5: HTTP 200 is returned with a grounded answer, a structured context payload, correct chunk counts, and user-friendly citations when retrieval data is available.

column2: RAG query success for doctor role with stricter citations
column3: Backend running; retrieval data exists; Gemini configured
column4: 1. Send POST /api/v1/rag_query with role=doctor. 2. Inspect answer format and citations. 3. Verify doctor-facing evidence traceability is stronger than the user role.
column5: The response remains grounded but uses the doctor-oriented citation style, including more explicit source detail and clinical traceability where available.

column2: RAG query rejects invalid query text
column3: Backend running; request validation enabled
column4: 1. Send POST /api/v1/rag_query with empty, whitespace-only, and very short queries. 2. Inspect response codes. 3. Verify no retrieval or LLM work is attempted.
column5: All invalid queries are rejected with HTTP 422 before expensive pipeline work begins.

column2: Gemini failure returns fallback answer without breaking the API
column3: Backend running; Gemini service intentionally fails or returns blocked/empty output
column4: 1. Trigger POST /api/v1/rag_query under a forced Gemini failure condition. 2. Inspect response body. 3. Confirm overall API status remains usable by the UI.
column5: HTTP 200 is still returned with a fallback answer and llm_error details, allowing the frontend to render context rather than failing hard with 5xx.

column2: Environment snapshot resolution and cache invalidation
column3: Environmental data table and Open-Meteo integration available; cached environment row exists
column4: 1. Call the environment-aware RAG path with current coordinates close to the cached coordinates. 2. Repeat with coordinates beyond the invalidation threshold or with a conflicting city label. 3. Observe selected environment block.
column5: Nearby coordinates reuse the cached snapshot, while materially different coordinates or city mismatch invalidate stale data so wrong location context is not sent to the model.

column2: Prompt safety when environment data is missing
column3: Backend running; no valid environment snapshot available
column4: 1. Send POST /api/v1/rag_query without usable environment context. 2. Inspect model output or fallback response text. 3. Confirm no weather or AQI facts are invented.
column5: The response explicitly avoids guessing local environment conditions and states that environment data is unavailable when the context is null or missing.

column2: Wearable vitals ingestion success
column3: Backend running; wearable/Health Connect payload available for a valid user
column4: 1. Send POST /api/v1/ingest/vitals with valid user_id and vitals data. 2. Verify rows written to the wearable vitals store. 3. Re-submit the same payload if idempotency is expected.
column5: Wearable vitals are accepted, linked to the user, and persisted in the expected structure without corrupting or duplicating data unexpectedly.

column2: Wearable vitals summary and reminder computation
column3: Backend running; enough vitals history exists for the user
column4: 1. Call GET /api/v1/vitals/{user_id}/summary. 2. Inspect trend summaries and reminder fields. 3. Compare with stored wearable samples.
column5: The summary endpoint returns coherent trends and reminder-ready insights derived from the user's wearable data, not unrelated or cross-user data.

column2: Automated data retention policy enforcement
column3: Retention migrations and cleanup jobs configured; old environmental and wearable rows exist
column4: 1. Insert environmental data older than 3 days and wearable data older than 30 days. 2. Run the cleanup job or retention process. 3. Inspect remaining rows.
column5: Expired rows are deleted according to the documented retention periods, while fresh records and clinically important current data remain intact.

column2: Dashboard endpoint full success response
column3: Backend or mock server running; user has dashboard, alert, and optional environment data
column4: 1. Call GET /api/v1/dashboard/{user_id}. 2. Inspect status, score, trend, alerts count, and environment block. 3. Compare with the API contract.
column5: The dashboard returns a valid success payload with the documented schema and correct user-specific metrics.

column2: Dashboard partial-success and zero-state handling
column3: Mock server or backend supports partial data responses; user has missing environment or no recent reports
column4: 1. Request dashboard data for a partial-data or empty-state user. 2. Inspect API payload and, if applicable, UI rendering. 3. Verify graceful handling of null environment and low-data states.
column5: The system returns partial_success or an equivalent safe response, with null or empty optional sections handled gracefully instead of crashing.

column2: Doctor patients endpoint success and risk-level integrity
column3: Backend or mock server running; doctor has linked patients
column4: 1. Call GET /api/v1/doctor/patients?doctor_id=... . 2. Inspect patient summaries, age, name, risk level, and last_updated. 3. Verify only the doctor's patients are returned.
column5: The endpoint returns the correct patient list with low/medium/high risk values and no leakage of unrelated patients.

column2: Android adapter maps HTTP and network failures to ApiResult.Error
column3: Android adapter layer tests or MockWebServer available
column4: 1. Simulate network failure, timeout, malformed response, and HTTP 4xx/5xx in the adapter layer. 2. Observe HealthApiAdapterImpl results. 3. Verify repository and ViewModel receive structured errors.
column5: Each failure mode is converted into ApiResult.Error with a useful descriptive message, rather than crashing or leaking raw exceptions to the UI layer.

column2: Android UiState handling covers loading, error, empty, and success states
column3: Android UI build or component tests available
column4: 1. Drive Dashboard, Alerts, and Assistant view models through loading, error, empty, and success conditions. 2. Observe ExampleActivity or screen composables. 3. Verify retry actions where documented.
column5: Each screen renders the correct state composable or inline state treatment for every UiState branch, including retry behavior and empty-state UI.

column2: Android report upload flow and timeline prepend
column3: Android app build available; backend /reports/process reachable
column4: 1. Open the Upload tab. 2. Select a document, toggle AI or Standard extraction, and submit. 3. Track UiState from Idle to Uploading/Processing to Success, then return to Dashboard.
column5: The upload screen follows the documented UiState flow, the processed report detail is shown, and the new report is prepended to the dashboard timeline without breaking existing navigation.

column2: Profile update, account deletion, and location-aware user management
column3: Backend and Android integration available; authenticated user exists; optional location permission flow available
column4: 1. Update profile fields through the profile-management flow. 2. Verify PATCH /api/v1/users/{user_id} succeeds. 3. Trigger account deletion and confirm token clearing. 4. Run a location-aware RAG query with location available and unavailable.
column5: Profile updates persist correctly, account deletion clears auth state and redirects safely, and location-aware flows degrade gracefully when location is missing instead of failing.
